sentences.text: Well, clothing was not really a major concern because you had no place to be or anything.
You couldn't afford anything.
You had no place to wash anything because there was no warm water.
There was no soap.
I managed to get from some sort of a-- I don't know, it must have been soda or some sort of a detergent.
And people that didn't wash got lice, and lice spread typhus, so-- or tee-phus of whatever.
You know, not typhoid.
That's a different kind of thing.
So I was very fanatical about washing.
So I used to wash-- they had these trough basins with a tap, you know, on top and cold water ran out.
And they were out in the open.
And you either had a choice of getting typhus or, or washing.
So I washed.
I was about 17 years old.
I was an innocent Jewish little girl from a good home, and I had to wash.
And if you washed yourself over here, the lice still went on the rest of you.
You have to wash everywhere.
So I would sit in there and wash in the ice cold water with the freezing thing with this soda, caustic type of gritty detergent.
And, and, and the guy who was standing there was washing his dishes.
I says, don't look at me.
I have no other place to wash.
He says, I'm not looking.
I don't mind.
I mean, I was pretty to look at, and he couldn't control himself.
What, what are you going to do?
So you say, how did you live?
You lived, and you dealt with it.

It had nothing to do with it with your inner self, because when I came out of there, I was just as innocent as when I went in, because I had decided I wasn't going to sell my-- sell myself for a piece of bread, which I had the opportunity many times to do.
And I did not because I chose not to.
It had nothing to do with wanting the bread or not.
I chose to think of myself as something else.
But this is not-- that's just about the washing.
So, so there were-- there were other things that happened.
I was working very hard.
I had worked very hard.
And I was a very pretty girl.
I'm-- now I'm an old lady, but once upon a time, I was a very pretty girl.
And, during-- and my father lived someplace else, and I lived in the barracks.
And I-- Oh, apropos, did I tell you in the barracks, my aunt lived there too, my father's oldest sister.
And she was a fantastic woman.
She was so sure that she was there to save the whole world.
Her husband was a very big man.
He weighed over 200 pounds, and she was a little tiny little woman.
Her name was Berthe, and they called her [NON-ENGLISH] because of her daughter.
And, and she gave him all her food.
She voluntarily got herself tuberculosis because she felt he was a big man.
And the little rations that we-- she got, she would say she already ate, and she would give it to her because she was-- he would come to visit her.
And she would prepare his food.
You know, they would eat together.
From wherever he was sleeping, he would come to her room.
There, she was the oldest in the room, so they, they-- she had some sort of a thing.
She gave out the food cards once a month and the margarine rations.
They knew she wasn't going to steal anything.
And she wasn't-- you know, she was going to give every-- she wasn't going to say it fell down or she-- they only brought five instead of 17 or something like that.
So she was doing that.
So he came to her place.
She was there.
She didn't have to go to work.
She was already-- oh, 40.
She was 56, 50-something. 93, '43-- well, she was 60, 50, whatever.
So and she gave him her food.
And at the end of the war, she had a pretty good case of tuberculosis.
Anyway, so there were-- there were things.
So I was there.
And in the evening, I would usually go to my father.
And my father would sometimes get a package from somebody.
And I would go when I could, in the after work and after the evening, especially in the-- in the months where it was light outside, and go and say, do you have any fatherly feelings for me?
Sometimes he'll give me a half-- a little bit of bread or sometimes he had a half a lump of sugar, whatever.
And what I was asking for was food, and if he had something, he would give me one bite of something.
So then-- so one day we were going along on the main street, and we were walking.
And I was walking with my father.
And the head of the-- of the Jewish council there-- you know, from the Nazis.
What, what, what they were there for was they got-- they got the number, and they had to give them-- they got a number, and they were told to give them 1,000 names for next Thursday.
And that's what they had to do.
They had a list of everybody, that's what they were there for, to win out who was going to go on the next transport, and things like that.
And the head of the Jewish council at that time-- there had been others before, one of them was called-- it was a Czech man.
And they wanted something from him that he would not give them, or they wanted him to make-- I do-- I honestly do not remember.
I used to know it once, but I'm sorry.
I've forgotten it.
And they put him on an iron bed, the Nazis, put him on an iron bedstead, and they poured gasoline over him and they set him on fire.
Epstein, his name was, or, or Echst-- I don't-- anyway, whatever.
And this-- he was one of the-- so you could be a hero, even if you were doing work like that.
I mean, you didn't-- it wasn't required of you, but you didn't have to be.
Mr.
Murmelstein, who was a rabbi from-- as it happened, it's a small world-- from, from Vienna, where I had grown up.
I knew him-- I knew him-- I knew him quite well, because I also know him aside from in his official function as a rabbi at the youth services in the temple.
My-- the parents of one of my girlfriends had a Jewish kosher restaurant.
And he would come every Saturday afternoon to eat fish and challah, you know, jellied carp and things.
So I knew him from there.
He would come and eat, and he never paid for anything.
And he would eat three plates full of food.
Anyway, he was very corpulent.
He was a big man.
And he had a little Van Dyke type of beard.

He was a young man, but he looked like-- because he was so fat and he wore a beard, you didn't realize that he was a man who was maybe in his 30s, maybe in Theresienstadt he was-- by that time he was 40.
Maybe he wasn't.
I honestly don't know, because to me, everybody who was over 15 was a grown up, and that was a different ball game.
So we were walking along the street and somebody was coming along, and it was Mr.
Murmelstein.
And Mr.
Murmelstein had a reputation.
He liked young girls.
How did he have this reputation?
How did you know about this?
By indulging himself, and everybody knowing about it.
How?
Because everybody knew this.
He likes young girls.
Well, if you do something all the time and you're forever seen with young girls, and there is another young girl a week later.
People were living on top of one another, 30 people in a room like this.
So he had a room by himself, but everybody could see.
He wasn't living in a house someplace.
There were 15 other people in that hallway, one cubicle next to the other.
It was a room, so it was the size of my bathroom.
So he had a bed in there, probably king size because he was so wide.
But still, that's it.
So everybody saw the girls going in there and coming out there, and I'm sure he didn't make a secret of it.
I don't know.
You knew everything about everybody.
Have you been in Israel?
People go out in the morning and they say to the neighbors, say good morning.
You pass on the balcony.
How am I doing today?
The neighbor knows better than you do what's going on.
Anyway, so, so all of a sudden, he waves to my father.
And my father says, just a minute, you know, and I stand there.
And he waves to my father to come over.
And my father says, just a minute.
I don't know what the idiot wants from me.
And he goes over to him.
You know, like, it was a wide street, and we were walking in the middle.
And, and he says to-- my father told me afterwards.
He says to him, which-- where did you get this?
He didn't recognize me.
But that time I was eight years old, and now I was already-- how old was I, 17?
I mean, it was a big change.
He says, where did you get this one?
I don't know that one.
He says, what do you mean this one?
That's my daughter.
He says introduce-- no, where did you get this one?
I don't know that one.
Introduce me to her.
He says, what do you mean, he said.
That's my daughter.
He says, so introduce me to her.
My father told him what he could do with himself.
It was very crude, and, and, and told him to leave him alone and went with me.
And told me, he says-- I said, you should have introduced me.
I would have taken care of him.
Don't worry.
Anyway--
On the main street.
This was the building where the officers were of this council, and this was where my father lived in this, this barr-- this horse stable thing.
Armory, that's what it was.
I couldn't think of the word.
You know, this armory like square block kind of things with open halls.
The stables were downstairs.
The rooms were upstairs, but everybody lived downstairs in the stables with the great archways and the big hallways.
So that's where we're walking.
People walked in the evening.
There was no place to go, and that was the only wide street where you didn't fall-- that you didn't-- other streets were narrow, and there were people all over the sidewalk and everywhere.
There was no room to walk. 60,000 people into 3,000 people, and there's no place to sit down, because all you have a pallet with straw on them.
So if you have a chance and it doesn't rain, and you're not too tired to fall down, you go out in the street, and you walk up and down.
That was our only entertainment.
There were no books.
There were no papers.
There were no pencils.
There was no soap.
There was no-- there was no anything.
You can't imagine it, but there was nothing.
What I have told you-- this kind of food, everybody had one dish and one spoon and one fork.
Nobody had any dishes.
Nobody had a cupboard.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Bare, bare, bare bones.
Sometimes not even the bones.
OK?
So and that was in September of 1944.
It was almost the last transport because the Russians were coming closer, and there were not so many transports going.
There were not two or three a week.
There was only one a week, because it was getting tir-- difficult to get through, because the Russians were advancing.
And a week later, I got a notice that I was in the transport.
Mr.
Murmelstein had resented my father, so he was going to show him who was the boss.
Because otherwise, I would never have been in there because none of us were ever there because of [?
Ram ?].
I mean, I don't know what he did, but he did it.
None of us ever got a thing.
I was the only one.
And the only-- I put that together myself.
But that is the only way my name would have gotten on there, because my father said so too.
Because my father wouldn't introduce him, so he couldn't have me.
Because [?
Ram ?] had said that this family, whichever way, or whenever the list came up to him, he took it out.
So he-- I was-- I got a little slip of paper about this big, one inch by, by three inches.
And it said my name and says report for the transport.
So the first thing I did, I went to my father where I knew he was working, and I said, I got this.
He says, you stay here, don't move.
And he went to the kommandatur and said to ask for-- and he was let in because he was doing work there all the time.
So they knew him, and he knew others two.
The second in command, his father had had a hardware store in Vienna.
And my father used to buy from his father.
So he was a customer of his father's in Vienna, so he knew him too.
He used to come sometimes to where my father worked, and they used to share a cigarette.
But he'd still hit people over the head with his oaken stick and killed a few people, but my father was the [NON-ENGLISH] because they knew him from before.
And anyway, so he went to the kommandatur and said-- he told me, he said, Obersturmfuhrer, my daughter's in the transport.
He says, I can't do anything today.
He says, tell her to disappear for two days.
And when that-- after the transport is gone, I will-- I will see that she gets other food coupons.
And that's it.
So my father said to me-- he came back and he said, it's all right.
You don't have to go there.
But you have to disappear.
I said, where should I go?
He says, I don't know.
Figure-- find a place.
So where should I go?
If I go back to my room, the people in the room, when they come to look for me, if I'm not show-- if I don't show up, when they'll check the list, they will say, there she is.

Or anybody that will see me, because they will realize that there is a body short, so anybody that knows where I am that has seen me will give me up, because if they don't find me, they might take him.
And when it gets to survival, none of the people that were sent away were ever heard from.

We didn't know they were dead, but we, we didn't think anything good was happening to them, because the condition under which they left and the type that nobody was allowed to talk about it, and things like that.
It didn't bode for anything good.
So [SIGHS] you know how it is.
People always have it-- you, you-- I didn't know where to go.
So I decided that there were people that helped the people onto the trains, the people that couldn't walk in the transport.

In that-- in that armory building where the train-- where the-- outside, where they had made the tracks in the street, there were people that-- young people that helped all the people to step up and to go down the steps or to get up from the stretchers, whatever.
They helped the, the the infirm.
So I decided-- they wore white scarves, babushkas, you know, a triangle thing.
I didn't have a white scarf, but I had a very, very, very pale champagne-colored silk scarf.
I don't know where I had-- this was something that I traded for something or other because I liked it.
It was beautiful, and I'd rather not eat and have one beautiful thing.
So I had that scarf.
And I decided the best thing to do was to go in there and hide in plain sight.
I figured it out.
Nobody was going to look for me in there because I wasn't where I was supposed to be.
And there was a four story building with two courtyards and four sides all around with open hallways and milling people and crying people and hysterical people and sleeping people all over the place.
Nope, it was-- it wasn't-- there was no danger.
So I decided to go in there, and I was going to become a helper.
But of course, I made myself scarce.
But I was walking around in there.
And then somebody says, you help those people.
He called me over, hey you help-- he was speaking German, and I speak Viennese.
So I said to him in Viennese, yeah, yeah.
And away.
So I had to help a few people.
And they were loading st-- you know, they were loading stretchers.
And they had stretchers lined up along the sidewalk, along the-- along the building.
And they had little low windows, you know cellar windows, just-- I don't know, like this and about a foot high against the ground.
And they were-- And so I helped them.
And so I spoke the Viennese dialect.
And I heard [INAUDIBLE] standing over by one of the things.
And he says, we don't have enough people.
We need another 16 or 18.
And the guy next to him says, he says, let's take the helpers.
They're standing around over here.
Let's take them in.
And I said, oh my god.
My father is going to kill me if he finds out I went into the-- into this place and they were able to grab me.
He said I should disappear.
So I was frantic, and I didn't know what to do.
So I, I looked around, and I couldn't see anything.
I couldn't see any place to go.
I was exposed.
But nobody was looking at me.
There was a lot of people milling about between me and all these people that were lying on the stretchers.
So I decided, I dropped down to the floor.
And I figured for the-- well, I'm standing up.
Maybe they'll find-- so I rolled under one of the stretchers.
It was good.
I was very skinny.
I couldn't do that today.
So I rolled under one of the stretchers, you know, that wasn't too low to the ground, and I laid there.
And, and then all of a sudden, I hear somebody say, all right, load the rest of the stretchers, you know?
Load the-- I said, that's it.
I'm dead.
The only thing I was worried about is my father is going to be angry.

I was terrified of what was going to happen to me, but I was afraid of my father, because I knew that if he ever caught me after that, I was going to pay for it dearly, because he'd never forgive me for being so stupid and not, not doing what he told me to do.
That was unacceptable.
He says, with life and death, you listen.
Anyway, so I, I don't know what I did.
I was going back.
And I said, I don't know.
Well, whatever.
I wasn't meant to die.
I moved my head.
I was wiggling, and the window behind me gave.
And I wiggled myself back and wiggled myself back and into the window.
And I turned myself around.
At least I didn't fall backwards.
And I got there, and the window gave.
I pushed it in.
And it was pitch black.
And I, I, I said, well, if I go there, I'll be dead.
And  if they don't kill me, Papa will.
So I might as well go in there.
And I thought I was going to break my neck or something.
I don't know what was going to happen.
And I fel-- and I launch-- launched myself in there, and I fell about this much.
There was a pile of coal there.
And I fell onto the coal, and I pushed the window closed.
And I laid there.
And I heard them loading the stretchers.
And I heard the train pull out, and it got night.
And I was there.
And I heard the rats and the mice.
And I was terrified, and I was crying.
And I was afraid to make a noise.
I was afraid somebody was patrolling outside, might hear me cry.
And I was-- it was a very difficult night.
And I was there.
And, and, and when the morning came, the train had pulled out, and I opened up and I looked around.
There was nobody.
And I went to my father's place, and I was lucky nobody see me.
And he says, what happened?
I was black.
I had a light beige coat on and this, this pale cream colored, champagne colored scarf.
Well, anyway, I was still alive, and I was crying and dirty and everything.
So then they let me stay over there because nobody had seen me come in until the afternoon, and then my father went and got me some food coupons.
And then I went back.
And they said-- and when I came back to my room, you know, I was legit, so I could go back.
Nobody was going to come look for me.
And that was the last transport.
Nobody-- nothing went after that anymore.
So they said, where were you?
I said, I had to do something.
He said, they came looking for you.
You were supposed to  be on the transport.
They came.
They were here three times.
Where were you?
I said, well, I'm here now.
OK.
That was in October.
So then we-- things went on.
Could I ask a general question?
Yes.
I've heard about Theresienstadt that it was an unusual place.
Theresienstadt was a, a propaganda item.
Theresienstadt was put there because whatever went on any place else was not fit for publication.
I mean, whatever monsters they were, they were not big enough to stand up and say, we hate the Jews, and we want to kill them like dogs.
They wanted everybody to think they were wonderful people.
They wanted to be loved, even for being nothing.
Anyway, so they, they built this up, and there was at one point a Red Cross commission.
And they, they invited them to show that the Jews had a wonderful life.
And they made-- actually they made a movie.
I'm in the movie, but I have been trying for 50 years to get a strip of it.
Somebody in the center in Brandeis gave me a strip, but I'm not on there.
I have a three, five minute piece of tape from the movie.
The-- it was called-- the movie was called Bad Theresienstadt.
Bad is like a spa, a Theresienstadt spa.
That's, you know-- that's, that's what it was.
And they had a main street.
People were scrubbing the sidewalks on their hands and knees.
They had a main street.
They made a butcher shop.
They made a millinery shop.
There made-- there were store fronts there.
There had been storehouses.
People were living in them.
They, they, they cleared out all the things.
That was still while the transports were going.
That was earlier, what I'm talking-- what they're talking about, the Red Cross commission.
That was a few months before.
They cleared out all the old people.
That's why they had the stretchers, the old, the infirm, so they wouldn't be able to see so many people in the street.
They shipped out, I don't know, 20, 25,000 people.
We were 60,000, then we were only 35,000, and so that the streets wouldn't be as crowded.
And we were not allowed to walk around the day that they were there anyway.
Only if it was absolutely essential.
So here and there, people-- some people were told to go get this or that.
And they had a coffee house.
Actually, the coffee house was there while we were there.
And we used the Theresienstadt money to go there.
They had shoes.
That many, some Jews had the instrument that they had brought and they hadn't taken them away from them.
And they would play.
And there were some very good musicians there.
And there were some movie directors.
There was a-- one was a German director from [NON-ENGLISH].
His name was Kurt Gerron, and he was the director of the-- of the movie for the Hitler thing.
And they made a movie.
They showed us.
They took us to the SS swimming facility on the Ager River.
And they made us go in the water.
And they, they photographed us.
I was in that shot.
And they photographed young girls working, you know, like in the agricultural department.
They went out with the wagons, and they were doing something.
And they photographed them doing that.
And they photographed people going to the coffee house.
And they photo-- they built a band shell in the main square.
And they had a band there, and it was playing in the evening.
They played nice stuff, Offenbach and stuff, very good.
It was nice.
But it was only for a couple of days, and then it was over.
And they had a butcher shop with real-- with real, honest to goodness sausages and meat hanging in the windows.
My uncle was lucky to go in-- to go in there.
And one day he managed to get-- he was a big man, you know, that Albert.
And he managed to get a bologna and put it into one of his sleeves, and he came home like that.
And he had a bologna.
Anyway, nobody caught him.
Anyway, so that was the thing.
So they-- the Red Cross came and they took them.
And they took them to the children's home, and they made beautiful rooms with white muslin curtains-- not the-- nylon curtains, or the net curtains, and everything.
And they, they, they were too stupid-- they were too stupid to see it, or maybe they didn't care about the Jews.
They believed it.
[LAUGHS] It didn't.

There was absolute-- it was-- you know like when you go into, into like a, a house of horrors they paint all kinds of things, in the back there are all kinds of machinations and horrible things, but in the front it all looks like this and that?
It was only fronts, like in the movies.
They make fronts, and in back you have all kinds of garbage.
There were storefronts.
The people were told to stay inside the houses.
They only took them where they wanted to see.
They didn't take them to the side street.
They didn't take them to the street by the barracks where I lived, where the roads were not paved, and when it rained we walked ankle deep in mud.
And I mean ankle deep.
They took them into the few things and they showed it to them.
And they went home and they said that Hitler is not that bad to the Jews.
They, they showed it, that film.
And the film existed, and I was been trying for a long time to get myself something.
But I was never able to get that piece when I was, with the swimming scene.
If you ever hear of anybody or you have any access to anybody with the swimming scene, it would be a lifetime dream realized if I could get just a second of that.
Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so that, that was Theresienstadt.
You worked.
In the evening you went for a walk.
Once a week you went to the coffee house.
But there were, of course, a lot of things going on that were not-- people would read books.
Somebody that spoke Czech knew the guy that was living across the road outside of town because he was a cousin of his brother-- of his brother-in-law's shoemaker or something.
So you know, people would thing-- there were all these moats and underneath passages.
People would smuggle things in.
People would smuggle things out.
I was a child.
I, I didn't know anybody.
I learned the language, but I didn't speak it really, so I wasn't involved in all of this.
But there were things.
There were-- you know, you heard things, and, and things went on one day after the other.
You did what you had to do.
And whenever anybody--
And Mr.
Ram ?] sometimes there were special appells.
I don't know, maybe they wanted to make us feel miserable, or maybe they-- you know, where they would line everybody up and you had to be counted.
And he would-- he would come out there and he would stand there and he would scream.
He says, all you people have in your head is how to fill your bellies and how to get out of here.
But I can promise you, you're never going to get out of here.
Apropos never getting out of here, my father was working in a carpentry shop, and he managed to have my brother with him.
And at that point--
